text: The surgeon places two 5mm ports for access, with one assisting port and a camera holder. The spleen and bowel are visible in the surgical field. The procedure begins with a lateral colic incision, aiming to reach the kidney pedicle and then proceed cranially to visualize the adrenal gland.

The surgeon discusses the approach for adrenalectomy, highlighting the importance of identifying the pedicle of the renal artery and vein as landmarks. The colon is visible and being mobilized to facilitate access to the adrenal gland. The bipolar instrument from Carl Storz is shown, although it is currently not functioning.

The surgeon holds simple scissors in the right hand while waiting for the bipolar instrument to be connected. The camera displays a large spleen that needs to be mobilized before proceeding further with the dissection.

The surgeon mobilizes the spleen by freeing it from its posterior attachments, allowing it to rotate to the right and open the area around the adrenal gland. Scissors are used to avoid monopolar energy, minimizing the risk of bowel damage. The procedure is viewed through a 30-degree lens, providing a clear optical view of the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal gland, visible amidst surrounding structures. The procedure involves careful mobilization of adjacent anatomical landmarks, including the pedicle of the kidney and potentially the aorta or vena cava on the right side, to facilitate access to the adrenal gland. The spleen is highlighted as a critical structure to avoid injury, as uncontrolled bleeding may necessitate a splenectomy.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the upper pole of the kidney, ensuring careful handling to avoid injury to surrounding structures such as the pancreas.
The surgeon identifies the tumor and attempts to locate the vessels for adrenal section. Gerardo's fascia is visible, and the pedicle is being sought in the surrounding area.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal gland and a vessel, possibly the renal vein, on the right edge of the surgical field. The vessel appears to branch towards the mass, and the surrounding tissue layer is visible. The procedure is ablative, with no reconstruction planned.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein leading to the tumor and notes the presence of hard tissue, likely lymph nodes, near the upper pole of the kidney. The lens rotation alters the anatomical orientation, requiring careful navigation to avoid cutting into the tumor.

The surgeon applies hemolog clips to medium-sized vessels, using two clips for larger vessels near the adrenal gland. Scissors are used to cut the vessels. The surgeon identifies a large vessel, possibly an upper pole vessel to the kidney, and emphasizes caution to preserve the kidney's blood supply. The surgical field includes the adrenal gland and surrounding structures, with ongoing mobilization to clarify anatomical relationships.

The surgeon considers following the medial aspect and identifies a vessel, which may be a lymphatic vessel. Minor vessels are visible, and the surgeon uses an instrument to cut them. A clip is applied to a vein, and the surgeon aims to mobilize the area further for better orientation and visualization.

The surgeon mobilizes the tumor, revealing a large mass and a clear dissection plane. The spleen is adequately mobilized to access the area, and the kidney is visible. Small upper pole vessels are identified, which can bleed if not controlled. The surgeon uses coagulation and a smoke evacuation system to manage the surgical field. Lymph vessels are noted, and the anatomy is further examined.

The surgeon identifies the entire tumor and the healthy part of the adrenal gland. The metastasis is also visible. The patient is in a lateral position, providing a clear view of the kidney and pedicle. The artery is visible, and the surgeon notes the presence of a significant amount of fat. The psoas muscle is identified as a landmark, indicating the anatomy is clear.

The surgeon identifies lymph fluid and the medial vein of the adrenal gland, applying a hemolock clip to control the vein. The camera angle provides a clear view of the anatomy, facilitating precise dissection. Adhesions are visible, requiring careful manipulation to avoid damage.

The surgeon attempts to rotate the tumor, revealing remaining adhesions that are carefully addressed using a no-touch technique to avoid mobilizing tumor cells. The healthy adrenal gland is visible, with the main tumor mass located on the opposite side. Hemostasis is performed, and pressure is reduced. The surgeon uses forceps to assist in the procedure, discussing the advantages of different lens angles for better visualization in obese patients.
